coredata.dc:title: A polydopamine coated magnetic spiral switchable composite material for photothermal sterilization and dual-color fluorescence detection of food-borne pathogens
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Jiaqi
Li, Jin
Lin, Xianfeng
Kang, Lixin
Duan, Nuo
Wang, Zhouping
Wu, Shijia
coredata.dc:description: A multifunctional magnetic composite material (MSp@PDA) featuring with helical structure, superparamagnetism, photothermal properties, and fluorescence quenching capability was synthesized through sequential magnetization and in-situ polydopamine coating on the Spirulina surface, then applied for multi-scenario application of pathogens capture, sterilization, and sensitive detection. The pathogens capture and sterilization was investigated by loading bacterial broad-spectrum recognition molecule (MPBA) on MSp@PDA, the capture efficiencies of 91.6 % and 94.2 % were obtained for Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus in 15 minutes, respectively, with complete photothermal sterilization achieved within 4 minutes. The fluorescence real-time detection of E. coli and S. aureus was realized by connecting dual-color fluorescent carbon dots to MSp@PDA through specific aptamers (dCD-apt-MSp@PDA), the corresponding detection limits were 2 and 3 cfu·mL−1, respectively, with linear ranges of 101∼107 cfu·mL−1. The spiked recovery rates of target bacteria in actual samples were 97.4∼101.1 % and 98.2∼101.4 % for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The constructed capture, sterilization, and fluorescence detection methods demonstrated promising application value for on-site monitoring of food-borne pathogens in complex matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic composite material
Food-borne pathogens
Photothermal sterilization
Carbon dot
Sensitive detection sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rapid capture and photothermal sterilization based on MSp@PDA-MPBA
Simultaneous fluorescence detection based on dCD-apt-MSp@PDA
Principle of capture, photothermal sterilization, and fluorescence detection based on MSp@PDA
Characterization and property investigation of MSp@PDA
Optimization of MSp@PDA-MPBA for rapid bacterial capture and photothermal sterilization
Characterization of dCD-apt-MSp@PDA and feasibility verification of fluorescence detection
Optimization of fluorescence detection conditions and performance study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of multifunctional magnetic composite materials: SEM images of Sp, MSp, and MSp@PDA (a1, a2), (b1, b2) and (c1, c2).

Exploration of multifunctional magnetic composite materials' performance. Magnetic properties: hysteresis loop (a); actual separation effect image (b). Photothermal performance exploration: thermal imaging (c); photothermal heating curve (d); UV spectra of supernatant after PDA coated (e).

Sterilization of captured E. coli and S. aureus using the photothermal performance of composite materials. Colony images after different durations of NIR irradiation treatment (a) and survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus (b) and (c), with 105 cfu·mL−1 of bacterials and 2 mg/mL of MSp@PDA and MNP@PDA.

Characterization of dual-color carbon dots. TEM images (a), particle size distribution (b), and fluorescence spectra (c) of bCD; TEM images (d), particle size distribution (e), and fluorescence spectra (f) of yCD. Insets show the physical appearance of bCD and yCD under sunlight and UV light (365 nm).

Feasibility validation. Zeta potentials of bCDs and bCDs-apt 1 and yCDs and yCDs-apt 2 (a); laser confocal microscope images of CD-apt incubated with target bacteria (b). Feasibility of nanodetection probes bCD and bCD-apt 1-MSp@PDA and yCD and yCD-apt 2-MSp@PDA in homogeneous systems (c) and (d), with insets showing the physical appearance of fluorescence detection probes under 365 nm irradiation, at a bacterial concentration of 105 cfu·mL−1. Fluorescence lifetime decay of bCDs-apt 1 and bCDs-apt 1-MSp@PDA and yCDs-apt 2 and yCDs-apt 2-MSp@PDA (e) and (f). Fluorescence recovery amount of dCD-apt-MNP@PDA and dCD-apt-MSp@PDA systems after incubated with corresponding pathogens under 365 nm irradiation (g) and (h), with 105 cfu·mL−1of bacterials and 2 mg/mL of MSp@PDA and MNP@PDA.

Optimization and performance of detection conditions. Fluorescence emission spectra of mixtures of bCD-apt 1 and yCD-apt 2 at different mixing ratios under 365 nm excitation (a). Quenching effects of different amounts of MSp@PDA on dual-color carbon dot mixed solutions fluorescence (b). Influence of incubation time on fluorescence quenching and recovery of signal probes (c) and (d), with bacterial concentration set at 105 cfu·mL−1. Changes in fluorescence spectra of detection systems with different concentrations of E. coli and S. aureus (e) and standard curves of fluorescence intensity at 432 and 563 nm versus logarithm of target bacterial concentration (f).
Schematic diagram of material preparation (a) and rapid capture, photothermal sterilization (b) and simultaneous fluorescence detection (c) of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus.

head.ce:title: A polydopamine coated magnetic spiral switchable composite material for photothermal sterilization and dual-color fluorescence detection of food-borne pathogens

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, School of Food Science and Technology, International Joint Laboratory on Food Safety, Jiangnan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multifunctional magnetic composite material (MSp@PDA) featuring with helical structure, superparamagnetism, photothermal properties, and fluorescence quenching capability was synthesized through sequential magnetization and in-situ polydopamine coating on the Spirulina surface, then applied for multi-scenario application of pathogens capture, sterilization, and sensitive detection. The pathogens capture and sterilization was investigated by loading bacterial broad-spectrum recognition molecule (MPBA) on MSp@PDA, the capture efficiencies of 91.6 % and 94.2 % were obtained for Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus in 15 minutes, respectively, with complete photothermal sterilization achieved within 4 minutes. The fluorescence real-time detection of E. coli and S. aureus was realized by connecting dual-color fluorescent carbon dots to MSp@PDA through specific aptamers (dCD-apt-MSp@PDA), the corresponding detection limits were 2 and 3 cfu·mL−1, respectively, with linear ranges of 101∼107 cfu·mL−1. The spiked recovery rates of target bacteria in actual samples were 97.4∼101.1 % and 98.2∼101.4 % for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The constructed capture, sterilization, and fluorescence detection methods demonstrated promising application value for on-site monitoring of food-borne pathogens in complex matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food-borne pathogens exhibit a high degree of contagion in crops, posing significant risks of contamination throughout food production, processing, and transportation. In particular, food-borne diseases stemming from pathogens such as Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) have emerged as globally acknowledged concerns in food safety [1–3]. In food environments, various food-borne pathogens may coexist in the same food sample or food processing equipment surface at varying concentrations, pressing demands have been proposed on efficient sterilization and sensitive pathogens detection method, which would contribute to pathogens control and monitoring, further achieving food safety regulatory warnings.

The existing methods for pathogens control, including high-temperature and high-pressure sterilization, ultrasonic sterilization, and spraying of chemical disinfectants, all suffer from time-consuming processes and the use of environmentally unfriendly chemical agents [4,5]. Consequently, urgent attention is required to develop a highly efficient and environmentally friendly new sterilization method. Due to the high efficiency in the near-infrared region and the ability to resist inducing pathogen drug resistance, photothermal conversion materials have shown the advantages of interfering with the pathogens metabolic and disrupting bacterial structures with non-toxic pathways, thus they are advocated to be applied in the efficient and eco-friendly sterilization of pathogens [6–9]. In particular, polydopamine (PDA), functioning as a natural biomimetic polymer, exhibits facile adherence to surfaces of various shapes to form dense composite layers. Due to their melanin-like broadband light absorption properties, strong near-infrared absorption characteristics enable them to produce excellent photothermal effects [10,11]. Wang et al. [7] prepared a photothermal antibacterial electrospun film substrate (PAN@PDA@UiO-66-NH2), which achieved 100 % photothermal inactivation of E. coli trapped on the membrane surface after 6 minutes laser irradiation, demonstrating excellent photothermal sterilization performance.

Moreover, the serious threat posed by food-borne pathogens in the natural environment necessitates not only efficient sterilization but also sensitive detection to achieve food safety regulatory warnings [12,13]. However, existing multiple detection methods, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [14], real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) [15], and flow cytometry (FCM) [16], require pre-enrichment culturing and difficult to detect at low pathogen concentrations, making it increasingly challenging to meet the demands of real-time detection in food environments. Carbon dots (CDs), due to their excellent fluorescent properties, particularly their multicolor tunability, find application in bioimaging and pathogen detection [17,18]. Cui et al. [19] prepared multicolor fluorescent CDs and coupled them with aptamers, developing a fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of S. aureus based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Similarly, Robby et al. [20] prepared fluorescent CDs and cesium tungsten oxide with excellent FRET effect, and developed a fluorescent sensor for the real-time detection of E. coli and S. aureus. Overall, CDs have demonstrated excellent signal variation capabilities when integrated into FRET systems. In particular, as a catechol-containing polymer, PDA exhibits advantages such as excellent electron transfer capability, hydrophilicity, ease of preparation, and the potential for further modification of surface functional groups, showing significant potential in the construction of FRET-based sensors [21].

Efficient enrichment and separation techniques will contribute to better pathogens sterilization and detection. It is worth mentioning that magnetic nanoparticles commonly offer notable advantages for the swift enrichment of trace bacteria in food matrices thanks to their susceptibility to magnetic field manipulation [21,22]. Nevertheless, traditional solvent-thermal-synthesized magnetic nanoparticles (such as Fe3O4, γ-Fe₂O₃, CoPt) possess small particle sizes and a tendency to aggregate on the surface, posing challenges to achieving high enrichment efficiency for targets in limited quantities [23,24]. The inherent spiral structure of Spirulina can effectively prevent the aggregation of magnetic nanoparticles when they are adsorbed, facilitating a higher degree of nanoparticle dispersion and thereby increasing their specific surface area, therefore magnetic Spirulina promotes comprehensive enrichment and removal of pollutants in homogeneous systems [25–27]. Accordingly, Cai et al. [28] loaded Fe3O4 and MnO2 onto Spirulina to prepare a well-dispersed magnetic spiral microrobot, which was applied for the high-density adsorption and rapid removal of Pb2+ in wastewater based on fast magnetic response, with the adsorption capacity up to 245.1 mg/g.

Hence, a polydopamine-coated magnetic Spirulina switchable composite material (MSp@PDA) was prepared, possessing the following characteristics: (1) a large specific surface area of the Spirulina structure, with good dispersion; (2) superparamagnetism, enabling rapid separation in food matrices; (3) photothermal conversion capability, allowing efficient sterilization under NIR irradiation; (4) high fluorescence quenching performance, enabling the opening and closing of fluorescence detection. Particularly, multi-scenario application of pathogens capture, sterilization, and detection would be achieved by switching different recognition molecules on MSp@PDA, the photothermal sterilization method was established based on MSp@PDA combined with a bacterial recognition molecule (4-mercaptophenylboric acid, 4-MPBA) which can indiscriminately bind to bacterial cell walls, and fluorescence detection for E. coli and S. aureus was realized by connecting dual-color fluorescent CDs to MSp@PDA through specific aptamers. These methods were of great significance for expanding the application of sterilization and real-time detection methods for multiple pathogens.
The materials, apparatus, and bacteria cultivation in the experiment were provided in the supporting information.

Preparation of Magnetic Spirulina (MSp). Spirulina harvested from the culture medium was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 minutes to separate it. It was then washed three times with deionized water to remove the culture medium. The resulting precipitate was washed three times with a 5 % glutaraldehyde solution and subsequently freeze-dried for later use. The treated Spirulina (0.5 g) was immersed in a solution of FeSO4·7 H2O (10 mL, 0.25 M) and stirred at room temperature for 30 minutes. Then, a mixture of KOH solution (20 mL, 0.25 M) and NaNO3 solution (20 mL, 1 M) was thoroughly mixed. The FeSO4 solution containing Spirulina was added to the above mixture with mechanical stirring. The resulting gel solution was then transferred to a water bath at 90 °C for 30 minutes. The product MSp was collected by magnetic separation, washed with deionized water, and collected for later use.

Preparation of MSp@PDA. MSp (50 mg) and hydrochloric acid dopamine (25 mg) were mixed in a Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 mL, pH 8.5, 10 mM). The mixture was continuously and gently stirred at room temperature for 3 hours using mechanical stirring. Then, MSp@PDA (10 mg) was suspended in pure water (5 mL), and APTES (100 μL) was slowly added. The suspension was then shaken overnight on a shaker, and the product was collected by magnetic separation and suspended in a PBS (5 mL) solution.

Initially, 4-MPBA thiol groups (1 mL, 0.5 mg·mL−1) were activated with TCEP (100 μL, 10 mM) for 1 hour. Then, the activated solution was added to a suspension containing MSp@PDA (1 mL, 2 mg·mL−1) in Tris-HCl (pH 8.5, 10 mM). Subsequently, NaCl (1 M) was added to increase the NaCl concentration in the mixture to 150 mM. Finally, after overnight shaking incubation, the product was collected by magnetic separation and suspended in PBS solution for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid capture and photothermal sterilization based on MSp@PDA-MPBA
ce:section.ce:para: For the bacterial capture experiment, S. aureus and E. coli (105 cfu·mL−1) were separately incubated with MSp@PDA-MPBA (50 μg) in PBS solution at 37 °C for 15 minutes. The material-bacteria complexes were then separated using a magnetic rack, resuspended in PBS solution, and after suitable dilution, plated onto agar plates. After 12 hours of incubation at 37 °C, bacterial growth was recorded, and the capture efficiency was calculated based on colony counting results.

Photothermal sterilization experiments were conducted by irradiating the bacterial suspension (105 cfu·mL−1) captured by MSp@PDA-MPBA with a red light source (660 nm, 2.0 W/cm2) using a photothermal imaging system to record the temperature change over time. After photothermal sterilization, aliquots of the suspension treated with different durations of light exposure were spread onto solid LB agar plates (20 mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 12 hours to assess bacterial growth. Additionally, aliquots of the suspension were added to liquid LB medium (50 mL) for bacterial regrowth, followed by OD600 measurement after 12 hours of incubation at 37 °C. Bacterial viability was calculated using the formula: Bacterial viability = Ac / Ai * 100 %, where Ac represents the absorbance of the non-NIR irradiated solution after dilution and culture, and Ai represents the absorbance of the solution irradiated by NIR at different times after dilution and culture.
The sterilization application on the exterior surfaces of food processing equipment was provided in the supplementary information.
The preparation of dual-color carbon dots and the coupling steps with target bacterial aptamers were described in the supplementary information.

Preparation of dCD-apt-MSp@PDA. Surface amino-modified MSp@PDA (2 mg·mL−1, 25 μL) was mixed with a solution of dual-color carbon dots (dCD-apt) at a ratio of 1:2 (300 μL), and the total system was adjusted to 350 μL using 10 % PBS solution. The mixture was gently shaken at room temperature for 30 minutes, the fluorescent probes were magnetically separated from the mixed solution and then resuspended in 10 % PBS (300 μL). This process was repeated three times to ensure the complete removal of unreacted fluorescent carbon dots, thereby enhancing the specificity of the detection results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous fluorescence detection based on dCD-apt-MSp@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of E. coli and S. aureus mixed solution (25 μL each, total 50 μL) were added to the prepared dual-color fluorescence signal probe solution (300 μL). After thorough mixing, the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 30 minutes. Unbound signal probes were removed by magnetic separation to avoid interference. Finally, the dual-color fluorescence signal values in the supernatant were measured under a 365 nm excitation light source.
The steps for actual sample pre-processing and spiking detection were provided in the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of capture, photothermal sterilization, and fluorescence detection based on MSp@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 (a) illustrated the preparation process of polydopamine-coated magnetic spiral composite material. Firstly, Fe3O4 was chemically deposited on the surface of Spirulina, imparting Spirulina with superparamagnetic properties (MSp). Subsequently, polydopamine (PDA) with photothermal and fluorescence quenching properties was coated on the surface of MSp to prepare multifunctional magnetic composite material (MSp@PDA). For further surface modification, MSp@PDA was surface amino-modified using APTES.

Scheme 1 (b) illustrated the bacterial capture and photothermal sterilization applications of MSp@PDA-MPBA. The recognition molecules (4-MPBA) were modified on the surface of MSp@PDA. Due to the boronic acid in 4-MPBA, which can bind with peptidoglycans in bacterial cell walls, rapid and bacterial recognition, and binding can be achieved in the presence of E. coli and S. aureus based on magnetic response. After bacterial capture, the excellent photothermal effect of the PDA shell on the surface of magnetic composite material enables photothermal sterilization of both bacterial species under near-infrared light irradiation.

Scheme 1 (c) illustrated the dual-color fluorescence detection of bacteria by dCD-apt-MSp@PDA. E. coli and S. aureus aptamers were coupled on the surface of two carbon dots with blue and yellow fluorescence synthesized by solvothermal method (bCD-apt 1 and yCD-apt 2). Due to the negative charge on the surface of CDs-apt, these were loaded onto the surface of amine-modified MSp@PDA through electrostatic adsorption (dCD-apt-MSp@PDA). The interaction between CDs-apt and PDA resulted in energy transfer, quenching the fluorescence of the carbon dots. In the presence of both E. coli and S. aureus, the aptamers on the dual-color carbon dots will preferentially bind to the target bacteria, causing detachment from the surface of the MSp@PDA material, and the fluorescence of the carbon dots will be restored, thereby achieving simultaneous detection of both bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and property investigation of MSp@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of the magnetic composite material were explored by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The original Spirulina (Sp) exhibited a long spiral structure with a smooth surface [26] ( Fig. 1a2). After the process of magnetization based on chemical deposition, the magnetic Spirulina material retained the same spiral structure, but its surface became relatively rough due to the deposition of magnetic nanoparticles [29] (Fig. 1b2). The principle of the preparation of MSp based on chemical deposition was described in supplementary information. Upon in-situ polymerization of PDA on the surface of the obtained magnetic Spirulina composite material, MSp@PDA still maintained a spiral structure with a larger specific surface area, and the presence of a PDA shell layer on its surface was clearly observed (Fig. 1c2), it could be seen that Sp, MSp, and MSp@PDA all exhibited excellent dispersion, the corresponding electron microscope images were shown in Figure S1.

Furthermore, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Zeta potential changes of Sp, MSp, and MSp@PDA confirmed the sequential modification of the magnetic shell and PDA shell on the surface of Sp. As shown in Figure S2a, characteristic peaks at 1656 and 1550 cm−1 of Sp which served as the chemical deposition template, attributed to the stretching vibration of −NH2 [28]. The appearance of a characteristic peak at 595 cm−1 in MSp corresponded to the Fe−O bond, indicating the successful deposition of the Fe3O4 magnetic shell on the surface of Sp. In comparison with Sp and MSp, the C−O characteristic peak at 1284 cm−1 in MSp@PDA indicated the successful coating of PDA on the surface of MSp [30]. As illustrated in Figure S2b, the original Sp possessed a negative charge (Zeta potential: −6.34 mV) after glutaraldehyde treatment. Deposition of Fe3O4 on the surface of Sp resulted in an increase in the negative charge of the core-shell structure from −6.34 to −1.94. Subsequently, in-situ coating of the PDA shell layer on the core-shell structure surface decreased the surface negative charge of the composite structure from −1.94 to −8.31 mV. Further surface amino modification of MSp@PDA changed its charge from negative to positive, reaching 5.73 mV [31]. These experiments fully demonstrated the successful preparation of MSp@PDA.
To further explore the excellent properties of MSp@PDA, we prepared magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) using the same chemical deposition method without adding Sp as a control group for comparison.

Magnetic property investigation. The superparamagnetic properties of MSp and MNPs were analyzed by vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) within the range of −20,000 to +20,000 Oe external magnetic field. As shown in Fig. 2a and b, Sp itself did not possess magnetic properties, but MSp obtained by chemically depositing magnetic shells on its surface exhibited excellent magnetic properties [32], with a saturation magnetization of 72.51 emu·g−1. Although the saturation magnetization of MNPs was slightly higher at 77.06 emu·g−1, both materials demonstrated strong magnetic properties with no significant difference. Furthermore, both MSp and MSp@PDA showed rapid magnetic response within 30 seconds under an external magnetic field (Fig. 2b), indicating their ability to quickly and effectively magnetically separate and enrich target substances from complex samples.

Photothermal property investigation. Using an NIR light source (660 nm, 2.0 W/cm2) for irradiation, the temperature changes over time of the material suspensions and controls were recorded to explore the differences in photothermal properties among different composite materials. From Fig. 2c and d, it can be observed that within 5 minutes of NIR irradiation, the temperature of MSp solution increased from 20.9 °C to 56.8 °C, which was slightly higher than the temperature increase of MNP by 3.7 °C. In contrast, pure water as a control only increased the temperature by 0.4 °C. After coating with PDA, MSp@PDA significantly raised the temperature from 20.9 °C to 74.0 °C, which was 11.9 °C higher than the temperature increase threshold of MNP@PDA [33,34]. Additionally, the PDA content in the reaction supernatant after coating PDA on MNPs and MSp was measured [35]. The results showed that the PDA content in the supernatant of MSp after coating with PDA was slightly lower than that of the MNP reaction system, indicating that the PDA shell layer coated on MSp with a spiral structure was thicker, thus increasing its temperature increase threshold (Fig. 2e). These results not only indicated the successful coating of PDA on MSp but also demonstrated the excellent photothermal conversion ability of MSp@PDA. The photothermal conversion efficiencies for MSp@PDA and MNP@PDA were calculated to be 54.8 % and 45.6 % (according to the calculation formula of supplementary information), which indicated that MSp@PDA was more efficient than MNP@PDA in absorbing and converting the 660 nm laser energy into heat [11,36,37]. Furthermore, by investigating the photothermal stability of MSp@PDA (Figure S3), this study found that the photothermal performance of the material could still maintain excellent photothermal conversion ability within five usage cycles. In summary, compared to MNP@PDA, the prepared MSp@PDA exhibited better photothermal performance and was expected to be used for rapidly killing bacteria in contaminated samples, thereby improving the safety of food environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of MSp@PDA-MPBA for rapid bacterial capture and photothermal sterilization
ce:section.ce:para: The surface phenylboronic acid groups of 4-MPBA can form covalent bonds with the cis-diol groups of bacterial surface lipopolysaccharides or phosphoric acid, enhancing the recognition and separation ability of the materials with bacteria in complex matrices [38,39]. Therefore, to investigate the bacterial capturing efficiency of MSp@PDA and MNP@PDA modified with the recognition molecule (4-MPBA), E. coli and S. aureus (105 cfu·mL−1) were employed as the upper limit capturing models, and different material addition amounts and incubation times were explored under corresponding conditions. In the phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.4) system, after adding different amounts of materials to the bacterial solution, incubation was carried out at 37 °C for 30 minutes to achieve sufficient capturing efficiency. Subsequently, the material-bacteria complexes were separated using a magnetic rack, resuspended in PBS solution, and gradient-diluted to appropriate concentrations for plate coating, and the capturing efficiency was calculated based on colony counting results.

In the experiment of capturing E. coli (Figure S4a), when the amount of MSp@PDA-MPBA continuously increased to 50 μg, the capturing efficiency significantly improved, reaching 90.3 %. However, when the amount exceeded 50 μg, there was no significant improvement in capturing efficiency. On the other hand, for MNP@PDA-MPBA, the capturing efficiency reached 88.7 % only after the amount continued to increase to 60 μg. Therefore, we fixed the material addition amount at 50 μg and further investigated the optimal incubation time with bacteria. As shown in Figure S4b, when the composite material was incubated with E. coli for 15 minutes, the optimal capturing efficiency of MSp@PDA-MPBA was 91.6 %, higher than the 88.5 % capturing efficiency of MNP@PDA-MPBA.

In the experiment of capturing S. aureus (Figure S4c), similarly, when the amount of MSp@PDA-MPBA was 50 μg, the capturing efficiency reached 90.6 %. However, when the amount exceeded 50 μg, there was no significant improvement in capturing efficiency. Similarly, for MNP@PDA-MPBA, the capturing efficiency reached 84.8 % only after the amount continued to increase to 60 μg. Therefore, we fixed the material addition amount at 50 μg and further explored the optimal incubation time. As shown in Figure S4d, when the composite material was incubated with S. aureus for 15 minutes, the optimal capturing efficiency of MSp@PDA-MPBA was 94.2 %, significantly higher than the 81.4 % capturing efficiency of MNP@PDA-MPBA.

These results fully demonstrated that compared to MNP@PDA, MSp@PDA modified with 4-MPBA had a larger surface area due to its spiral structure [32], thus possessing a more abundant capturing molecule. As a result, it could more fully exhibit excellent capturing performance towards E. coli and S. aureus in homogeneous systems [13,24].

Utilizing NIR irradiation on re-suspended bacterial suspension post-capture (105 cfu·mL−1), and recording the temperature change over time using a photothermal imager. As depicted in Fig. 3a, the bacterial suspensions subjected to different durations of light exposure exhibited markedly reduced colony counts on agar plates compared to the control group, showing a rapid decline trend. The excellent sterilization effect was attributed to the modification of the composite material surface with 4-MPBA, capable of binding to peptidoglycans in bacterial cell walls, thereby tightly connecting bacteria to the composite material complex and inducing localized high-temperature sterilization upon light exposure. Moreover, owing to the higher temperature threshold and rate, MSp@PDA-MPBA demonstrated significantly superior sterilization efficacy compared to MNP@PDA-MPBA. Additionally, bacterial viability after varying durations of NIR irradiation, as shown in Fig. 3b and c, revealed that although the sterilization efficiency against S. aureus was slightly lower than that against E. coli, complete eradication of both captured bacteria was achieved after 4 minutes of NIR irradiation.

To further explore the practical application value of this composite material, we sprayed MSp@PDA-MPBA on the external surface of food processing equipment and investigated its sterilization effect using NIR irradiation for 4 minutes. As illustrated in Figure S5, complete sterilization of the external surfaces contaminated with E. coli and S. aureus was achieved after 4 minutes of NIR irradiation. In contrast, a substantial amount of bacteria remained on the external surfaces sprayed with pure water as the control group after NIR irradiation. These experimental results further validated the rapid temperature elevation and bacterial inactivation capability of MSp@PDA-MPBA after 4 minutes of NIR irradiation, highlighting its advantage as a photothermal sterilizing agent for environmentally friendly and non-destructive sterilization of food processing equipment exteriors.

Based on the aforementioned experimental findings, it was evident that utilizing MSp@PDA modified with recognition molecules for NIR photothermal treatment enabled the eradication of captured bacteria, thereby minimizing contamination and hazards posed by food-borne pathogens to food and the environment. Therefore, the application of MSp@PDA in detection was explored next.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of dCD-apt-MSp@PDA and feasibility verification of fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4a and d, the solvothermally prepared dual-color carbon dots (CDs) were spherical, with uniform size and good dispersion [17,40]. The average diameters of the blue and yellow fluorescence CDs were 4.68 ± 0.76 nm and 3.74 ± 0.61 nm, respectively (Fig. 4b and e). Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were used to demonstrate the optical properties of dual-color fluorescence carbon dots. As depicted in Fig. 4c and f, under excitation wavelengths of 350 and 410 nm, the maximum emission wavelengths of the two CDs were 432 and 563 nm, respectively. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence signals of the two CDs could be easily distinguished, and signal interference would not occur. The inset illustrated their bright fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. Additionally, we investigated the stability of the two CDs under 365 nm UV irradiation. As shown in Figure S6, the synthesized CDs exhibited excellent optical stability, with fluorescence intensity remaining stable after continuous 120 minutes of UV irradiation.

The coupling of aptamers with dual-color carbon dots was characterized via Zeta potential ( Fig. 5a). After coupling with aptamers via the carbodiimide method, the Zeta potentials of bCD and yCD changed from −8.81 mV and −6.78 mV to −15.73 mV and −13.62 mV, respectively, due to the abundant negative charges on the phosphate backbone of the aptamers. These results indicated the successful preparation of the bCD-apt 1 and yCD-apt 2 complexes. The specific targeting of aptamers to the target bacteria was directly confirmed by laser confocal scanning microscopy (Fig. 5b). After incubating with E. coli and S. aureus for 60 minutes, blue and yellow fluorescence signals were observed, respectively, from the carbon dots modified with the corresponding aptamers (bCD-apt 1 and yCD-apt 2). Thanks to the specific binding effect between the aptamers and the targets [41], simultaneous detection of two food-borne pathogens can be achieved by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of the two different-colored carbon dots.

To verify the fluorescence quenching performance of CDs-apt-MSp@PDA, the fluorescence intensities of the two CDs were studied at different stages. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, after adding MSp@PDA to both CDs-apt, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly (line c), while the fluorescence emission intensity only slightly decreased when CDs were directly mixed with MSp@PDA (line b). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the composite nanoprobes (CDs-apt-MSp@PDA) was noticeably restored after adding the corresponding pathogenic bacteria targets (line d). As shown in the inset, under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the fluorescence intensity in the solution gradually decreased in the order of lines a, b, d, and c, consistent with the results in the homogeneous system.

Figure S7 showed a significant overlap between the absorption spectrum of MSp@PDA and the fluorescence emission spectra of bCD and yCD, facilitating the triggering of the FRET effect between them [35]. To verify the quenching mechanism of FRET, the fluorescence decay spectra of the two CDs-apt with and without the addition of MSp@PDA were tested, and the corresponding fluorescence lifetimes were calculated. As shown in Fig. 5e and f, after mixing with MSp@PDA, the fluorescence lifetimes of bCD-apt 1 and yCD-apt 2 decreased from 7.66 ns and 3.98 ns to 4.51 ns and 3.72 ns, respectively, further confirming the existence of energy transfer process between the two CDs-apt and MSp@PDA, indicating the quenching mechanism as FRET effect [42,43]. Fluorescence recovery amount of dCD-apt-MNP@PDA and dCD-apt-MSp@PDA in the same quality after incubated with corresponding pathogens were measured (Fig. 5g and h), a more significant quenching effect was shown in dCD-apt-MSp@PDA system compared to dCD-apt-MNP@PDA system, and the corresponding recovery amount in dCD-apt-MSp@PDA system was considerably higher. This was due to the fact that rich inherent spiral structures exhibited in Sp, which would effectively prevent the magnetic nanoparticles aggregation and their specific surface area was increased, therefore promoting more PDA absorption, and under the same mass, the fluorescence quenching effect and subsequent recovery of dCD-apt-MSp@PDA were more significant compared to dCD-apt-MNP@PDA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of fluorescence detection conditions and performance study
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve simultaneous quantitative fluorescence detection of E. coli and S. aureus, it is necessary for the dual-color fluorescence carbon dots to exhibit multi-color emission under a single wavelength excitation for fluorescence quantitative detection. Since the excitation wavelengths for bCD and yCD were 350 nm and 410 nm, respectively, a unified excitation source (365 nm) was proposed. However, under 365 nm excitation, the fluorescence intensity of both bCD and yCD were inevitably affected, thus requiring further adjustment of the mixing ratio of bCD-apt 1 and yCD-apt 2 to make their fluorescence intensities close to each other. As shown in Fig. 6a, when the mixing ratio of bCD-apt 1 and yCD-apt 2 was 1:2, their fluorescence intensities were closest and the peak intensity was higher. Therefore, the mixing ratio was determined to be 1:2. Due to the presence of FRET between bCD and yCD and MSp@PDA, the fluorescence of the carbon dots could be effectively quenched by MSp@PDA. Fig. 6b showed the dCD-apt with the addition of different masses of MSp@PDA. After adding MSp@PDA, the fluorescence intensity of mixed solutions rapidly decreased. The quenching effect became slower after adding 50 μg of MSp@PDA. Therefore, the amount of MSp@PDA added was determined to be 50 μg. The reason for achieving higher quenching efficiency at a lower addition amount may be a direct result of the non-covalent binding of the aptamer on the surface of MSp@PDA and the energy transfer from the carbon dots to MSp@PDA. To achieve better sensitivity for subsequent detection, we further optimized the conditions for fluorescence quenching and recovery. Fig. 6c showed the influence of incubation time on the fluorescence quenching of dual-color carbon dots in the presence of MSp@PDA (50 μg). After adding MSp@PDA, the fluorescence intensity of the dual-color fluorescence peak rapidly decreased and continued to decrease within 30 minutes, with no significant quenching effect observed after 30 minutes. Therefore, the incubation time for fluorescence quenching was set at 30 minutes. Fig. 6d showed the influence of incubation time on the fluorescence recovery of MSp@PDA-dCD-apt in the presence of E. coli and S. aureus (25 μL each, total 50 μL). As shown in the figure, after adding the two target bacteria, the dual-color carbon dots loaded on MSp@PDA began to detach and bind specifically with the target bacteria through the aptamer. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity in the solution rapidly increased, with a significant increase within 30 minutes, and no significant recovery effect was observed after 30 minutes. Therefore, the incubation time for fluorescence recovery was set at 30 minutes.

The dual-color carbon dots were mixed with MSp@PDA under optimal conditions to prepare the fluorescence detection probe (dCD-apt-MSp@PDA). The signal probe was collected by magnetic separation and resuspended in 300 μL PBS solution. Then, different concentrations of E. coli and S. aureus mixed solution (25 μL each, total 50 μL) were added to the above detection system. After incubating at room temperature for 30 minutes, the unbound signal probe was removed by magnetic separation. Finally, the dual-color fluorescence in the supernatant was determined under a 365 nm excitation light source. As shown in Fig. 6e and f, with the increase in the concentration of target bacteria, the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant also increased continuously. Within the concentration range of 101∼107 cfu·mL−1, the fluorescence values of the fluorescence detection system showed good linear relationships with the logarithm of the concentrations of E. coli and S. aureus, with linear regression equations of y = 318.7x – 55.08 (R2 = 0.998) and y = 378.4x – 56.28 (R2 = 0.997), respectively. The corresponding detection limits were 2 and 3 cfu·mL−1, respectively. (3σ/k, where σ and k are the standard deviation of the blank group and the slope of the linear regression equation, respectively).

Compared with previously reported methods for detecting E. coli and S. aureus listed in Table S1, the method established in this study demonstrated better detection sensitivity and a wider linear range. Furthermore, previous methods mainly focused on the quantitative detection of single bacteria, while the fluorescence detection method developed in this study could instantly detect two pathogenic bacteria, thus expanding the application of rapid identification and detection methods for various pathogenic bacteria in complex food matrices. Moreover, the specificity of this method was examined by setting up seven control experiments. As shown in Figure S8, after incubating dCD-apt-MSp@PDA with different groups of bacteria for 30 minutes, although the concentrations of Listeria monocytogenes and Vibrio parahaemolyticus were one order of magnitude higher than those of E. coli and S. aureus, the presence of miscellaneous bacteria in different groups did not affect the fluorescence intensity of target bacteria detection. That was due to the high specificity binding ability of the aptamer with the target bacteria, thereby avoiding interference from miscellaneous bacteria. These results fully demonstrated the excellent specific recognition and detection capabilities of the dual-color fluorescence aptamer sensor proposed in this study for E. coli and S. aureus.

To test the detection performance of the sensor, conventional plate counting method and the proposed fluorescence detection method were used to simultaneously detect the contents of E. coli and S. aureus in artificially contaminated samples. The results were summarized in Table S2, and there were no significant differences between the results obtained by the developed fluorescence detection method. The recovery rates of E. coli and S. aureus in the actual samples were 97.4∼101.1 % and 98.2∼101.4 %, respectively, thus proving that the aptamer sensor developed in this study can be used for simultaneous determination of E. coli and S. aureus in complex food samples.

In summary, multi-scenario application of pathogens capture, sterilization, and detection were achieved by switching different recognition molecules on the prepared MSp@PDA, the sterilization relied on the photothermal properties of the MSp@PDA and indiscriminate adsorption of 4-MPBA to bacteria. Under optimal conditions, capture rates of 91.6 % for E. coli and 94.2 % for S. aureus were obtained within 15 minutes, respectively, a complete sterilization was obtained in 4 minutes. In addition, fluorescence detection for E. coli and S. aureus was realized based on the fluorescence quenching capability of MSp@PDA and the specific recognition of aptamers. The linear ranges spanned from 101 to 107 cfu·mL−1, with detection limits of 2 and 3 cfu·mL−1. The spiked recovery rates in actual samples were 97.4–101.1 % for E. coli and 98.2–101.4 % for S. aureus.

The current study not only proposed an efficient, non-destructive sterilization method using only photothermal irradiation without the need for high-temperature sterilization equipment or chemical reagents, but also provided a basis for rapid and sensitive approach for on-site real-time detection of E. coli and S. aureus. Furthermore, the detection of a wider range of multiple pathogenic bacteria would benefit from the current materials and methods by matching corresponding recognition molecules. These efforts contributed to the establishment of a safety control system for food and environmental detection processes.

Shijia Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Nuo Duan: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Zhouping Wang: Validation, Resources. Lixin Kang: Software. Xianfeng Lin: Data curation. Jin Li: Data curation. Jiaqi Feng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFF1103000), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32072310), the Distinguished Young Scholars of Jiangsu Province (BK20240100), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP202404019), and the Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136935.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jiaqi Feng received his B.S. degree from Wuhan Polytechnic University in 2020 and his Master’s degree from Wuhan Polytechnic University in 2023. Currently, he is a Ph.D. student in Jiangnan University. His research focuses on the synthesis of sensors based on functional composite materials and their application in food safety detection and control.

Jin Li received his Master’s degree from Jiangnan University in 2024. His research interests are the synthesis of sensors based on functional composite materials and their application in food safety detection and control.

Xianfeng Lin received his B.S. degree from Jiangxi Agricultural University in 2019 and his Master’s degree from Jiangnan University in 2022. Currently, he is a Ph.D. student in Jiangnan University. His research focuses on the synthesis of functional materials and their application in food safety detection and control.

Lixin Kang received his B.S. degree from Bohai University in 2020 and his Master’s degree from Jiangsu University in 2023. Currently, he is a Ph.D. student in Jiangnan University. His research focuses on the synthesis of functional materials and their application in food safety detection and control.

Nuo Duan received her Ph.D. degree in Food Science from Jiangnan University in 2014. Now she is a Professor in the School of Food Science and Technology, at Jiangnan University. Her research interests are focused on aptamer selection and aptamer-based analytical application in analytical chemistry and food safety control.

Zhouping Wang got his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Southwestern University in 2004 and post-doctorate from Tsinghua University. Now he is a Professor in the School of Food Science and Technology, at Jiangnan University. He is interested in the development of aptasensors and their application in detection systems.

Shijia Wu got his B.S. degree from Wuhan Polytechnic University in 2008 and the Ph.D. degree from Jiangnan University in 2013. He worked as a visiting scholar at University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign in 2018. Now he is a Professor in the School of Food Science and Technology, at Jiangnan University. He is interested in the development of functional material, biocatalysis, aptasensors used in detection systems, and their application in food safety control. To date, he has published more than 80 articles in major international journals and he has a total impact factor greater than 600, more than 6900 citations, and an H-index of 48.
